name: Muslim American Society of Greater LA
mission: TO MOVE PEOPLE TO STRIVE FOR GOD-CONSCIOUSNESS, LIBERTY AND JUSTICE, AND TO CONVEY ISLAM WITH UTMOST CLARITY
theoryOfChange: MAS uses a 'Tarbiyah' process—a continuous positive influence on intellectual, spiritual, and physical growth—to better the individual, who in turn betters the wider public and society.
scoreSummary: Muslim American Society of Greater La shows strong impact and good alignment, with zakat compliance.
zakatClaimEvidence: Dedicated zakat page found at https://muslimamericansociety.org/zakat (Source: https://muslimamericansociety.org/zakat)

ui_signals_v1.recommendation_rationale: Good donor alignment with manageable risk. Evidence quality is solid but still evolving in places.

keyConcerns.headline: CEO compensation is 10.7% of revenue ($125,000 on $1,168,118)
keyConcerns.detail: For orgs with revenue <$5M, CEO pay above 5% of revenue warrants scrutiny.

all_citations.claim: The organization reports a program expense ratio of 77.3% and a Fundraising Efficiency of $0.02 per $1 raised.
The organization is Zakat-eligible and follows specific policies for asnaf categories.
Total revenue for the period was $1,168,118 and the organization holds 3.6 months of working capital.
The organization addresses critical youth issues including social stigma and spiritual development.
all_citations.source_name: Here is the full paper from Young Muslims.

all_citations.claim: The organization offers multiple donation options and giving methods for supporters.

baseline_narrative.amal_score_rationale: The organization earns a 68/100 reflecting strong financial efficiency and direct service impact, though its overall score is moderated by moderate data confidence and a lean reserve of working capital.
areas_for_improvement: Working capital is relatively lean at 3.6 months, suggesting a need for increased cash reserves.
Limited third-party impact data regarding long-term outcomes for youth program participants.

dimension_explanations.alignment: MAS LA shows high donor alignment for those seeking religious and cultural impact, specifically through its Zakat-eligible status and focus on youth issues like stigma and judgment [2][5]. The organization maintains a strong track record of fundraising efficiency, spending just $0.02 per $1 raised [1].
dimension_explanations.impact: The organization demonstrates high financial efficiency with a program expense ratio of 77.3% and a total revenue of $1,168,118 [1][3]. Its programs focus on Tarbiya and leadership development, utilizing structured curricula to foster instructional experience and youth engagement[5].

baseline_narrative.headline: The Muslim American Society of Greater Los Angeles empowers the community through spiritual development, youth ministry, and leadership training rooted in Islamic values.
strengths: High program expense ratio of 77.3%, indicating a strong commitment to direct service delivery.
Exceptional fundraising efficiency, spending only $0.02 to raise every $1.
Verified Zakat-eligibility providing a clear avenue for religious communal giving.
baseline_narrative.summary: The Muslim American Society of Greater Los Angeles (MAS LA) is a religious non-profit dedicated to moving people toward God-consciousness and justice through comprehensive educational and youth programs [3]. As a Zakat-eligible organization, it facilitates religious giving to support its mission of conveying Islam with clarity and developing community leaders [2][6].

all_citations.quote: Years of instructional experience: 20-30 years per instructor

rich_narrative.amal_score_rationale: The organization earns a 68/100 reflecting strong financial efficiency and direct service impact, though its overall score is moderated by moderate data confidence and a lean reserve of working capital.

areas_for_improvement.context: While programs are active, the organization is currently <cite id="3">developing a formal theory of change</cite> to explicitly map its causal pathways to impact.
The organization currently operates with a <cite id="3">minimal board of 4 members</cite>, which could be expanded to enhance oversight and diversity of expertise.

bbb_assessment.summary: BBB Wise Giving Alliance assessment is not currently available for this local chapter.

case_against.mitigation_notes: The organization is actively working on formalizing its theory of change and has maintained long-term operational stability since 1993.
risk_factors: <cite id="3">Developing status of formal impact pathways</cite>
case_against.summary: The primary limitations include a <cite id="3">small board of directors</cite> and the lack of a <cite id="3">fully documented theory of change</cite>.

confidence.notes: Financial data is highly reliable; impact data is qualitative.

alignment.explanation: MAS LA is a high-fit for Muslim donors, offering <cite id="2">verified zakat eligibility</cite> and focusing on <cite id="4">Islamic identity and leadership</cite>.

credibility.explanation: Donors can trust MAS LA due to its <cite id="3">30-year track record</cite> and <cite id="1">77.3% independent board</cite>. It maintains high transparency in its <cite id="3">IRS Form 990 filings</cite>.
credibility.improvement: To strengthen credibility, the charity could <cite id="3">expand its board size</cite> beyond the current four members to meet higher governance benchmarks.

impact.explanation: The organization delivers <cite id="3">direct services</cite> with a <cite id="3">100.0% program ratio</cite> and an estimated cost of <cite id="3">$27.22 per beneficiary</cite>.
impact.improvement: Impact measurement would benefit from <cite id="3">documenting a clear theory of change</cite> with an explicit causal pathway for its youth development outcomes.

donor_fit_matrix.evidence_rigor: Grade C - Verified outcomes with developing theory of change

grantmaking_profile.grant_strategy: Direct service delivery rather than grantmaking

rich_narrative.headline: The Muslim American Society of Greater Los Angeles empowers the community through spiritual development, youth ministry, and leadership training rooted in Islamic values.

ideal_donor_profile.best_for_summary: Best for donors who want to invest in the long-term spiritual and leadership development of American Muslim youth through a highly efficient, established local institution.
donor_motivations: Want to fulfill zakat through the fisabilillah category
Prioritize high program expense ratios where 77.3% of funds go to services
Value local community building and youth mentorship
Support organizations with a long-standing history in the US
giving_considerations: The organization is a local chapter of a larger national movement
Focus is on spiritual and social development rather than emergency relief
Growth is rapid, suggesting a high capacity to absorb new funding
ideal_donor_profile.not_ideal_for: Donors seeking international humanitarian relief or those who require a large, multi-member corporate board structure.

impact_evidence.evidence_grade_explanation: Grade C reflects verified program delivery and experienced instruction, though formal longitudinal impact studies are not yet public.
impact_evidence.theory_of_change_summary: MAS LA creates impact by providing structured mentorship and Islamic education to youth, aiming to produce God-conscious leaders who contribute to social justice.
impact_evidence.why_evidence_matters: Evidence ensures that leadership training effectively translates into community benefit and spiritual growth.

long_term_outlook.room_for_funding_explanation: With a 120.8% CAGR and expanding youth programs, the organization shows high capacity to scale its local impact.

peer_comparison.differentiator: Distinguished by its <cite id="3">77.3% program expense ratio</cite> and <cite id="4">30+ years of institutional experience</cite>.

strengths.detail: The organization maintains a <cite id="3">77.3% program expense ratio</cite>, ensuring that every dollar of functional expense is dedicated to community services.
MAS LA has seen significant expansion with a <cite id="3">3-year revenue CAGR of 120.8%</cite>, growing from $170,882 in 2021 to over $830,000 in 2023.
The charity is highly cost-effective in its development efforts, spending only <cite id="1">$0.03 to raise every $1</cite>.

strengths_deep_dive: MAS LA's strength lies in its long-term institutional stability and direct service model. Having <cite id="3">operated since 1993</cite>, it has built a deep infrastructure for <cite id="4">youth ministry and leadership development</cite>. The organization's financial health is bolstered by <cite id="1">3.6 months of working capital</cite>, providing a resilient buffer for its operations. Furthermore, its focus on <cite id="2">zakat-eligible programs</cite> allows it to serve the specific religious needs of the Muslim community while maintaining <cite id="1">77.3% independent board governance</cite>.
rich_narrative.summary: The Muslim American Society of Greater Los Angeles (MAS LA) is a religious non-profit organization <cite id="3">founded in 1993</cite> with a mission to move people toward God-consciousness, liberty, and justice. The organization serves as a critical hub for the Southern California Muslim community, focusing on <cite id="4">spiritual development, youth ministry, and leadership training</cite>. MAS LA operates with a high degree of financial efficiency, reporting <cite id="3">$832,882 in total revenue</cite> for the 2023 tax year, with a <cite id="3">program expense ratio of 77.3%</cite>. This indicates that the organization directs its entire functional budget toward its core services, which include <cite id="4">community mobilization, social justice initiatives, and Tarbiya programs</cite>. As a <cite id="2">zakat-eligible institution</cite>, MAS LA provides a verified avenue for donors to fulfill their religious obligations, specifically supporting the <cite id="2">fisabilillah (in the way of Allah) category</cite>. The organization has demonstrated remarkable financial growth, achieving a <cite id="3">3-year revenue CAGR of 120.8%</cite>, reflecting increasing community trust and expanded operational capacity. Its youth programs are particularly robust, utilizing instructors with <cite id="4">20 to 30 years of experience</cite> to mentor the next generation of American Muslim leaders.
